one_line_takeaway: Stripping overt 'triggering cue' language from AdvBench/HarmBench/StrongREJECT prompts while preserving malicious intent turns near-total refusals (ASR ~4-11%) into near-total compliance (ASR 87-100%) across 10 LLMs including Gemini 3 Pro and Claude Sonnet 3.7/4, showing these benchmarks -- and the safety alignment tuned against them -- largely detect surface phrasing rather than harmful intent.
key_finding: Removing triggering cues alone (First Revision) raises mean ASR from 3.86% to 87.54% on AdvBench and from 10.55% to 79.10% on HarmBench, and the effect generalizes to StrongREJECT (a dataset not used to design the method), where mean ASR rises from 3.54% to 88.06%. With the iterative revision-regeneration jailbreak loop, ASR climbs further to 90.00%-100.00% across all 10 models on all three datasets within a few iterations, including models the paper characterizes as among the safest evaluated (Gemini 3 Pro, Claude Sonnet 3.7/4). Practicality/applicability of the resulting harmful responses stayed high throughout (mean PE 98.08%-100.00% across tables), indicating the unsafe outputs were not merely abstract but contained transferable real-world detail. Separately, the dataset-quality analysis found over 45% of AdvBench data points near-identical at a 0.95 similarity threshold and over 11% at 0.99, versus far lower duplication in a size-matched GSM8K subset (e.g., at 0.85 threshold only ~11% of AdvBench points are unique vs. ~94% of the GSM8K counterpart; HarmBench shows 16% duplication vs. 3.5% for its GSM8K counterpart at the same threshold).
core_contribution: The paper (Golchin & Wetter, Labelbox Applied ML Research) makes two linked claims about widely used LLM adversarial-safety benchmarks (AdvBench, HarmBench) and, by extension, StrongREJECT: (1) in isolation, these datasets are dominated by 'triggering cues' -- overt negative/sensitive words and phrases (e.g. 'commit suicide', 'without getting caught', 'tutorial', 'step-by-step instructions') that are unrealistic compared to how real adversaries phrase requests, and the datasets contain heavy near-duplication (measured via Sentence-BERT all-MiniLM-L6-v2 pairwise cosine similarity against a size-matched GSM8K baseline); and (2) in practice, once these cues are stripped out via a technique the authors call 'intent laundering' -- an LLM-driven rewriting procedure with two components, connotation neutralization and context transposition, implemented via an 8-shot in-context-learning 'intent launderer' (GPT-5.1) -- attack success rate against 10 target models jumps dramatically, showing that model refusals on the original benchmarks were being driven largely by surface-level triggering language rather than by robust detection of the underlying malicious intent. The authors further extend intent laundering into a standalone black-box jailbreak by adding an iterative revision-regeneration loop that uses prior failed revisions as feedback, achieving 90.00%-100.00% ASR across all 10 models within a few iterations, including on Gemini 3 Pro and Claude Sonnet 3.7/4.
threat_model: Fully black-box adversary with only API/query access (no weights, no logits) to a target LLM. The adversary starts from a known malicious data point (from AdvBench/HarmBench/StrongREJECT), rewrites it via an LLM 'intent launderer' to remove overt triggering language while preserving intent (and, in the jailbreak variant, iteratively regenerates the rewrite using an LLM judge's pass/fail feedback), then submits the rewritten prompt directly to the target model. Success is defined by an LLM-judge scored on two independent axes -- safety (5-point Likert: extremely unsafe to safe) and practicality/real-world applicability (5-point Likert: extremely applicable to inapplicable) -- both conditioned on preserving the original malicious intent; ASR requires both an unsafe AND a practical response.
limitations: The paper's own scope notes: analysis is restricted to two seed datasets (AdvBench, HarmBench) plus one generalization check (StrongREJECT); topic diversity of the datasets is explicitly excluded from the critique (only data-point-level duplication and triggering-cue overreliance are analyzed). All LLM-judge scoring (both intent laundering and the underlying safety/practicality evaluation) is delegated to GPT-5.1 as an automated judge, with human-agreement figures relegated to an appendix rather than the main results reported here. The intent launderer itself is a single model (GPT-5.1) with an 8-shot ICL setup; the paper does not report ablations of the launderer model or ICL demonstrations within the paper. The revision-regeneration jailbreak experiments explicitly do not apply the paper's own defined termination conditions (target ASR or max iterations) but instead run a fixed representative number of iterations to characterize the trend, which the authors flag themselves as a deviation from the deployable jailbreak procedure described in Section 3.3.

datasets_used: AdvBench (Zou et al., 2023) -- 520 original data points; 207 unique after 0.9-similarity deduplication
HarmBench standard split (Mazeika et al., 2024) -- 200 unique data points used (all of the deduplicated set)
StrongREJECT (Souly et al., 2024) -- 313 data points total, minus 25 overlapping with AdvBench, leaving 288 used
GSM8K (Cobbe et al., 2021) -- used only as a non-safety baseline for the duplication analysis, size-matched via random subsampling to each safety dataset
quantitative_results: AdvBench mean ASR: 3.86% (No Revision) -> 87.54% (First Revision) -> 96.47% (Third Revision, best/bold)
HarmBench mean ASR: 10.55% (No Revision) -> 79.10% (First Revision) -> 91.60% (Fifth Revision, best/bold)
StrongREJECT mean ASR: 3.54% (No Revision) -> 88.06% (First Revision) (further iterations partially reported)
Final-iteration jailbreak ASR range across all 10 models and 3 datasets: 90.00%-100.00%
AdvBench near-duplication: >45% of points near-identical at 0.95 cosine-similarity threshold; >11% at 0.99 threshold
At 0.85 similarity threshold: ~11% of AdvBench points unique vs. ~94% of size-matched GSM8K subset
At 0.85 similarity threshold: HarmBench 16% duplicated vs. 3.5% for its GSM8K counterpart
Per-model No-Revision ASR on AdvBench ranges 0.00% (GPT-4o, Gemini 2.5 Flash) to 17.87% (Grok 4); on HarmBench 0.50% (GPT-4o, Gemini 2.5 Flash) to 36.00% (Grok 4)
Mean ASR increase from first to final iteration: +9.00 points on AdvBench, +11.81 points on HarmBench, +9.89 points on StrongREJECT
baselines_compared: No-Revision setting (original benchmark prompts, standard ASR) serves as the paper's own baseline against which First/subsequent Revision ASR is compared
GSM8K (size-matched subsamples) used as a non-safety-dataset baseline for the duplication/uniqueness analysis, not as a jailbreak baseline

10 target models compared against each other under identical intent-laundering treatment: Gemini 3 Pro, Claude Sonnet 3.7 (Sonnet 4 for StrongREJECT), Grok 4 (non-reasoning), GPT-4o, Llama 3.3 70B, GPT-4o mini, Qwen2.5 7B, GLM 5.1, Kimi K2, Gemini 2.5 Flash
